expanded_description: Master the three fundamental trigonometric ratios—sine, cosine, and tangent—that relate acute angles to side length ratios in right triangles, and apply the SOH CAH TOA mnemonic to solve for missing sides and angles in real-world applications.

core_concept.explanation: Trigonometry studies the relationships between angles and side lengths in triangles. In right triangles, three specific ratios—sine (opposite/hypotenuse), cosine (adjacent/hypotenuse), and tangent (opposite/adjacent)—remain constant for any given angle measure, regardless of triangle size. The mnemonic SOH CAH TOA helps remember which sides correspond to which ratio.

image.alt: Right triangle with angle θ labeled, sides labeled opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse, with three ratio formulas displayed
image.caption: Each trigonometric ratio compares two specific sides relative to the reference angle

sections.section_title: Identifying Sides: Opposite, Adjacent, and Hypotenuse

image.alt: Two identical right triangles shown side-by-side with different reference angles marked, showing how opposite and adjacent sides switch positions
image.caption: Opposite and adjacent depend entirely on which acute angle you're referencing

sections.introduction: Before applying trig ratios, you must correctly identify which side is opposite, which is adjacent, and which is the hypotenuse relative to your reference angle. This is the most critical foundational skill in trigonometry.

key_concept.definition: In a right triangle with reference angle $\theta$: Hypotenuse is always opposite the right angle (longest side). Opposite side is directly across from angle $\theta$. Adjacent side touches angle $\theta$ and is not the hypotenuse.
key_concept.context: The hypotenuse never changes—it's always the longest side opposite the $90^\circ$ angle. However, opposite and adjacent swap positions depending on which acute angle you choose as your reference.

image.alt: Triangle with angle A marked, sides labeled; then same triangle with angle B marked, showing opposite/adjacent labels swapped
image.caption: Changing the reference angle flips the opposite and adjacent designations

principles.statement: The labels 'opposite' and 'adjacent' are relative to the chosen reference angle. The hypotenuse remains constant.
principles.application: Always mark your reference angle first before labeling sides. Draw an arrow from the angle to the opposite side to avoid confusion.
principles.example: In $\triangle ABC$ with right angle at $C$, if reference angle is $A$, then side $a$ (BC) is opposite and side $b$ (AC) is adjacent. If reference angle is $B$, then side $b$ is opposite and side $a$ is adjacent.
principles.analogy: Like sitting at a table: the person directly across from you is 'opposite,' the person next to you is 'adjacent.' Change your seat, and these relationships change.

image.alt: Step-by-step diagram showing angle marking, then side labeling with arrows
image.caption: Systematic labeling prevents side identification errors

worked_examples.title: Labeling Sides for Different Reference Angles
worked_examples.problem: In right triangle $XYZ$ with right angle at $Z$, $XY = 13$, $YZ = 5$, and $XZ = 12$. Label the sides as opposite, adjacent, or hypotenuse relative to angle $X$, then relative to angle $Y$.
worked_examples.final_answer: Relative to $\angle X$: opp=5, adj=12, hyp=13. Relative to $\angle Y$: opp=12, adj=5, hyp=13

image.alt: Triangle XYZ with both labeling scenarios shown side-by-side
image.caption: Opposite and adjacent swap when reference angle changes

image.alt: Right triangle with angle θ, showing three ratio formulas with highlighted sides for each
image.caption: Each ratio uses a different pair of sides to create a unique relationship with the angle

sections.introduction: Once sides are identified, the three trigonometric ratios provide three different ways to relate the reference angle to side lengths. Each ratio serves different problem-solving purposes.

key_concept.definition: For acute angle $\theta$ in a right triangle: $\sin \theta = \dfrac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$ (SOH). $\cos \theta = \dfrac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$ (CAH). $\tan \theta = \dfrac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}}$ (TOA).
key_concept.context: These ratios are constant for any given angle measure. A $30^\circ$ angle always has the same sine, cosine, and tangent values regardless of triangle size. This property enables us to solve for unknown sides or angles.

image.alt: Three separate triangles each highlighting the two sides used in one ratio, with formula displayed
image.caption: SOH CAH TOA mnemonic encodes which sides belong to which ratio

principles.statement: For any fixed angle measure $\theta$, the values of $\sin \theta$, $\cos \theta$, and $\tan \theta$ are constant, regardless of triangle size.
principles.application: This allows us to use calculators or tables to find ratio values for any angle, then apply them to triangles of any scale.
principles.example: $\sin 30^\circ = 0.5$ for every right triangle with a $30^\circ$ angle, whether the hypotenuse is 2 cm or 200 km.

image.alt: Three similar right triangles of different sizes, all with 30° angle, showing same ratio values
image.caption: Similarity ensures trig ratios depend only on angle measure, not triangle size

principles.statement: The tangent ratio represents the slope or steepness of the angle: $\tan \theta = \dfrac{\text{rise}}{\text{run}} = \dfrac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}}$.
principles.application: Tangent is especially useful for incline problems, ramp design, and any situation involving slope or grade.
principles.example: A $45^\circ$ angle has $\tan 45^\circ = 1$, meaning rise equals run—a 100% grade or 1:1 slope.

image.alt: Right triangle positioned as a ramp with rise and run labeled, tangent formula shown
image.caption: Tangent directly measures steepness as a ratio of vertical to horizontal change

worked_examples.title: Calculating All Three Trig Ratios
worked_examples.problem: In right triangle $ABC$ with right angle at $C$, $AC = 8$, $BC = 15$, and $AB = 17$. Find $\sin A$, $\cos A$, and $\tan A$.

image.alt: Triangle ABC with all sides labeled, three ratio calculations displayed
image.caption: All three ratios use the same triangle but different side pairs

sections.section_title: Using Trigonometric Ratios to Find Missing Sides

image.alt: Three scenarios showing: given angle and hypotenuse find opposite (sine), given angle and hypotenuse find adjacent (cosine), given angle and adjacent find opposite (tangent)
image.caption: Choose the ratio that includes both the known side and the unknown side

sections.introduction: When you know one acute angle and one side length, trigonometric ratios allow you to find any missing side. The key is selecting the ratio that contains both the known side and the unknown side.

key_concept.definition: To find a missing side: (1) Identify the reference angle, (2) Label known and unknown sides as opposite, adjacent, or hypotenuse, (3) Choose the ratio (SOH, CAH, or TOA) that includes both the known and unknown sides, (4) Set up the equation and solve algebraically.
key_concept.context: This is the most common application of trigonometry. Unlike the Pythagorean Theorem which requires two known sides, trig ratios need only one side and one angle.

image.alt: Flowchart showing decision process for ratio selection based on which sides are known/unknown
image.caption: Ratio selection depends on the positions of known and unknown sides

principles.statement: Choose the trigonometric ratio that contains both the given side and the side you're solving for.
principles.application: Given angle and hypotenuse, finding opposite? Use sine. Given angle and adjacent, finding hypotenuse? Use cosine. Given angle and opposite, finding adjacent? Use tangent.
principles.example: Given $\theta = 40^\circ$ and hypotenuse = 10, find opposite. Use sine: $\sin 40^\circ = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{10} \Rightarrow \text{opp} = 10 \sin 40^\circ \approx 6.43$.

image.alt: Decision matrix showing which ratio to use for each combination of known/unknown sides
image.caption: This matrix eliminates guesswork in ratio selection

worked_examples.title: Finding Opposite Side Using Sine
worked_examples.problem: In right triangle $PQR$ with right angle at $R$, $m\angle P = 35^\circ$ and hypotenuse $PQ = 20$ cm. Find the length of side $QR$ (opposite to $\angle P$).

step_by_step.explanation: Identify known and unknown sides relative to $35^\circ$
Choose ratio containing opposite and hypotenuse

worked_examples.concept_applied: Sine ratio to find opposite side

image.alt: Triangle PQR with angle 35°, hypotenuse 20, opposite side QR labeled with calculation
image.caption: Sine relates the known hypotenuse to the unknown opposite side

worked_examples.title: Finding Adjacent Side Using Tangent
worked_examples.problem: A ladder leans against a wall at a $70^\circ$ angle with the ground. The ladder reaches 15 feet up the wall. How far is the base of the ladder from the wall?

step_by_step.explanation: Model as right triangle and identify sides
step_by_step.calculation: Angle = $70^\circ$. Opposite (height) = 15 ft. Unknown: adjacent (distance from wall).

step_by_step.explanation: Choose ratio containing opposite and adjacent

worked_examples.final_answer: The base is approximately 5.5 feet from the wall
worked_examples.concept_applied: Tangent ratio to find adjacent side

image.alt: Ladder diagram with 70° angle, height 15 ft, base distance x labeled with calculation
image.caption: Tangent relates the known height to the unknown base distance

sections.section_title: Finding Missing Angles Using Inverse Trigonometric Functions

image.alt: Right triangle with two sides labeled, showing inverse trig calculation to find angle measure
image.caption: Inverse trig functions reverse the process: given a ratio, find the angle

sections.introduction: When you know two side lengths but no angles (except the right angle), inverse trigonometric functions allow you to find the acute angle measures. These are written as $\sin^{-1}$, $\cos^{-1}$, and $\tan^{-1}$.

key_concept.definition: Inverse trigonometric functions find the angle measure when given a ratio: If $\sin \theta = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\text{hyp}}$, then $\theta = \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\text{opp}}{\text{hyp}}\right)$. Similarly for cosine and tangent.
key_concept.context: The $-1$ superscript does NOT mean reciprocal—it means 'inverse function.' On calculators, these are often accessed via a '2nd' or 'shift' key. The result is an angle measure in degrees (or radians).

image.alt: Flow diagram showing side ratio being input to inverse function, outputting angle measure
image.caption: Inverse functions work backward from standard trig ratios

principles.statement: Choose the inverse trig function based on which two sides you know: opposite and hypotenuse → $\sin^{-1}$. Adjacent and hypotenuse → $\cos^{-1}$. Opposite and adjacent → $\tan^{-1}$.
principles.application: This mirrors the standard ratio selection but works in reverse to find angles instead of sides.
principles.example: Given opposite = 5 and hypotenuse = 13, find angle: $\theta = \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{5}{13}\right) \approx \sin^{-1}(0.3846) \approx 22.6^\circ$.

image.alt: Decision chart showing which inverse function to use based on known sides
image.caption: Same side-pair logic as standard ratios, but output is angle instead of ratio

worked_examples.title: Finding Angle Using Inverse Tangent
worked_examples.problem: In right triangle $ABC$ with right angle at $C$, side $AC = 7$ and side $BC = 10$. Find the measure of angle $A$.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify known sides relative to angle $A$
Choose inverse function for opposite and adjacent

worked_examples.concept_applied: Inverse tangent to find angle from two legs

image.alt: Triangle ABC with legs 7 and 10, angle A labeled with inverse tan calculation
image.caption: Inverse tangent converts the side ratio back to an angle measure

categories.description: Proper calculator usage is essential for accurate angle calculations.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Ensure calculator is in degree mode (not radian mode). Use the '2nd' or 'shift' key to access inverse functions (often labeled as $\sin^{-1}$, $\cos^{-1}$, $\tan^{-1}$ above the standard trig buttons). Enter the ratio value, not the angle.

image.alt: Calculator display showing degree mode indicator and inverse trig button sequence
image.caption: Wrong mode setting is the most common source of incorrect angle answers

examples.explanation: If $\sin^{-1}(0.5)$ gives you 30, you're in degree mode (correct). If it gives you 0.5236, you're in radian mode (incorrect for this course). Switch to degree mode before calculating.

image.alt: Two calculator screens: one showing 30° (correct), one showing 0.5236 (wrong)
image.caption: Degree mode ensures angle answers are in familiar degree measure

image.alt: Flowchart: Given angle and side? → Find missing side using trig ratio; Given two sides? → Find angle using inverse trig
image.caption: Systematic approach ensures correct function selection and accurate solutions

sections.introduction: Trigonometry problems follow predictable patterns. This workflow helps you identify what's given, what's needed, and which trig function to apply.

process_flow.description: Sketch the right triangle if not provided. Mark the right angle, label the given angle(s) and side(s), and mark the unknown with a variable.
process_flow.result: Clear visual representation of the problem

image.alt: Blank triangle being labeled with given information and unknown marked with x
image.caption: A well-labeled diagram prevents side identification errors

process_flow.process: Identify reference angle and side positions
process_flow.description: Choose the acute angle you'll work with (given or to be found). Label all three sides as opposite, adjacent, or hypotenuse relative to this angle.

image.alt: Triangle with reference angle circled and all three sides labeled with their positions
image.caption: Correct labeling is essential before choosing a trig function

process_flow.description: Finding a side? Choose SOH, CAH, or TOA based on which sides are known and unknown. Finding an angle? Choose the inverse function based on which two sides are known.
process_flow.mechanism: Match known and unknown elements to ratio components

image.alt: Decision matrix showing function selection based on known/unknown combinations
image.caption: This matrix eliminates guesswork in function selection

process_flow.description: Write the trig equation with known values substituted. Solve algebraically for the unknown. Use calculator for trig values or inverse functions. Round appropriately.

image.alt: Equation being solved step-by-step with calculator shown
image.caption: Algebraic manipulation isolates the unknown before calculation

worked_examples.title: Multi-Step Application: Finding Height of a Tree
worked_examples.problem: You stand 50 feet from the base of a tree. The angle of elevation to the top of the tree is $42^\circ$. Your eye level is 5 feet above the ground. How tall is the tree?

step_by_step.calculation: Right triangle formed by: horizontal distance = 50 ft (adjacent), tree height above eye level = $h$ (opposite), angle of elevation = $42^\circ$.

step_by_step.explanation: Choose ratio containing opposite and adjacent
Add eye level height for total tree height

worked_examples.final_answer: The tree is approximately 50 feet tall
worked_examples.concept_applied: Tangent ratio with real-world angle of elevation

image.alt: Tree diagram showing observer 50 ft away, 42° angle, eye level 5 ft, tree height calculation
image.caption: Real-world problems often require adding or subtracting baseline measurements

classifications.value: Mistakes that consistently cause incorrect trig calculations
characteristics: Using wrong reference angle for side labeling
Choosing incorrect trig ratio (mixing up SOH CAH TOA)
Calculator in radian mode instead of degree mode
Forgetting to isolate variable before calculating
classifications.behavior: Always double-check: (1) Is the right angle marked? (2) Are sides labeled correctly relative to the reference angle? (3) Does the chosen ratio contain both known and unknown? (4) Is calculator in degree mode? (5) Did you solve algebraically before calculating?
classifications.note: The most common error is using the wrong reference angle. Always circle or highlight your reference angle before labeling sides.

image.alt: Three error examples with red corrections showing proper technique
image.caption: Recognizing these patterns prevents systematic calculation failures

summary: Trigonometric ratios—sine, cosine, and tangent—relate acute angles to side length ratios in right triangles. SOH CAH TOA encodes these relationships: $\sin \theta = \dfrac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$, $\cos \theta = \dfrac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$, $\tan \theta = \dfrac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}}$. To solve problems: (1) identify the reference angle, (2) label sides as opposite, adjacent, or hypotenuse, (3) choose the ratio containing both known and unknown elements, (4) set up the equation and solve algebraically. Use inverse trig functions ($\sin^{-1}$, $\cos^{-1}$, $\tan^{-1}$) to find angle measures when two sides are known. Critical success factors include correct side labeling relative to the reference angle, proper calculator mode (degrees, not radians), and selecting the appropriate ratio based on known/unknown positions. These ratios are constant for any given angle regardless of triangle size, enabling applications in surveying, engineering, physics, and architecture. Mastering SOH CAH TOA provides the foundation for all advanced trigonometry and real-world angle-side calculations.
